qualities.definition: Characteristics or features that make someone the way they are.
qualities.example: There are four qualities we need to behave well.

model.definition: To show others how to behave by doing it yourself.
model.example: Your job is to model good behaviour for your child.

empathetic.definition: Able to understand and share other people's feelings.
empathetic.example: An empathetic parent tries to see things from the child's view.

socialized.definition: Able to get along well with other people in society.
socialized.example: These four skills help a child become socialized.

tolerate.definition: To accept something unpleasant without complaining.
tolerate.example: Children need to learn to tolerate frustration.

frustration.definition: A feeling of being upset because you cannot do what you want.
frustration.example: Learning to handle frustration is an important skill.

flexibility.definition: The ability to change and adapt to new situations.
flexibility.example: Flexibility means being open to changing your plans.

perspective.definition: A way of looking at something; a point of view.
perspective.example: Try to see things from your child's perspective.

expectation.definition: What you think or hope will happen.
expectation.example: I changed my expectation about how fast we would walk.

progress.example: Children make progress at different speeds.

mimic.definition: To copy someone's actions or behaviour.
mimic.example: Some children naturally mimic the people around them.

milestones.definition: Important events or achievements in development.
milestones.example: Children reach milestones at different ages.

fluency.definition: The ability to do something smoothly and easily.
fluency.example: I couldn't read with fluency until I was nine.

behavioural.definition: Related to how someone acts or behaves.
behavioural.example: Children develop behavioural skills at different times.

translates.definition: Means the same thing as; can be understood as.
translates.example: 'Driving me mad' translates as 'I cannot control the behaviour.'

whining.definition: Making a high, complaining sound because you are unhappy.
whining.example: The child was whining because she was tired.

demanding.definition: Always asking for things in a strong, forceful way.
demanding.example: A demanding child may just need more attention.

negative.definition: Bad or harmful; the opposite of positive.
negative.example: Getting negative attention is better than no attention for a child.

circumstances.definition: The conditions or facts surrounding a situation.
circumstances.example: Understanding the circumstances helps you understand the behaviour.

colic.definition: Severe stomach pain in babies that makes them cry a lot.
colic.example: Colic can make a baby very difficult to soothe.

discomfort.definition: A feeling of slight pain or being uncomfortable.
discomfort.example: Babies may cry because of discomfort from a full nappy.

nappy.definition: A piece of cloth or material worn by a baby to absorb waste.
nappy.example: Check if the baby needs a fresh nappy.

alternatively.example: Alternatively, your child might have difficulties later.

self-control.definition: The ability to control your feelings and actions.

soothing.definition: Making someone feel calm and less upset.
soothing.example: Soothing a child helps them move to the next stage.

master.definition: To learn to do something very well.
master.example: A child is most frustrated just before they master a new skill.

brittle.example: Children are at their most brittle before a breakthrough.

outburst.definition: A sudden, strong expression of emotion.
outburst.example: The child's outburst was a sign of frustration, not bad character.

tantrum.definition: A sudden explosion of anger, especially by a child.
tantrum.example: A tantrum means the child hasn't yet reached a developmental milestone.

sulk.definition: To be silent and unhappy because you are upset about something.
sulk.example: The child began to sulk when told she couldn't have the toy.

intentional.example: A tantrum is not an intentional plan by the child.

intervention.definition: Getting involved in a situation to change it.
intervention.example: The outburst is not a planned intervention by the child.

lax.definition: Not strict enough; too relaxed about rules.
lax.example: Lax parents can still have well-behaved children.

strict.definition: Having firm rules that must be followed.
strict.example: Strict parents may still have children who misbehave.

consistency.definition: Always behaving in the same way; being reliable.
consistency.example: Consistency in parenting helps children feel safe.

fairness.definition: Treating everyone equally and in a right way.
fairness.example: Children respond well to consistency and fairness.

sticker.definition: A small piece of paper with a picture that you can stick on things.
sticker.example: Sticker charts are a way of judging, not relating.

charts.definition: Tables or lists used to track and record information.
charts.example: Behaviour charts are manipulative and don't teach real skills.

bribes.definition: Gifts or rewards given to make someone do what you want.
bribes.example: Bribes should be the exception, not the norm.

manipulative.definition: Using clever or unfair ways to control people.

robots.definition: Machines that can do tasks automatically, without feelings.
robots.example: We want children to relate, not behave like robots.

collaboration.example: Collaboration leads to a more harmonious life.

harmonious.example: A harmonious home makes everyone feel better.

opposition.definition: Being against something or fighting against it.

motivated.definition: Having a reason to want to do something.
motivated.example: We want children motivated by empathy, not fear of punishment.

chores.definition: Regular tasks around the house like cleaning or cooking.
chores.example: Children learn to help with chores by imitating their parents.

dishwasher.example: Let toddlers play with the dishwasher to build interest.

cooperate.definition: To work together to do something.
cooperate.example: Children cooperate when they feel included and respected.

contribute.definition: To give or do something to help.
contribute.example: They help because they want to contribute, not for a bribe.

imitating.example: Children learn by imitating the adults around them.

gratitude.example: Children learn real gratitude when it is shown to them.

drill.definition: To make someone repeat something many times until they learn it.
drill.example: If you only drill your child to say thanks, they may never feel it.

embody.definition: To express something through your whole being, not just words.
embody.example: When children feel gratitude, they can truly embody it.

narcissism.definition: Caring too much about yourself and how others see you.
narcissism.example: Put your narcissism aside and focus on your child's feelings.

humiliate.definition: To make someone feel very embarrassed and ashamed.
humiliate.example: Don't humiliate your child by forcing them to say things.

unappreciated.example: Say thank you yourself so the giver doesn't feel unappreciated.

pretend.definition: Not real; made up as part of play.
pretend.example: Welcome those pretend cups of tea your child hands you.

invested.definition: Put time and effort into something for a good result later.
invested.example: Those hours playing with your child are invested hours.
